title: Ajahn Funn: In the past, people worried about this and that, couldn't go anywhere, only clung and grasped. Afraid of lacking, afraid of hunger, afraid…
content:
The Dead Don't Complain Even When Fire Is Piled On Them

In the past, people worried about this and that, couldn't go anywhere, only clung and grasped. Afraid of lacking, afraid of hunger, afraid of suffering, afraid of poverty. Now, they are not afraid of anything at all. Even when fire is piled on them, they don't cry out "Ouch!" even once. We should contemplate to know and see like that, so we won't be deluded. This is the Dhamma teaching. The living don't listen. When performing bang-sukun, they say they are "pulling" for the dead to go to heaven or nibbāna. Hey, how would they know?

When a person is about to die, coldness rises from the feet, from the arms and hands, while the head grows cool. They all converge at the heart. If that person has merit, has built merit, the merit goes to produce a new birth. Why would it stay? Why would it stay in that coffin? If it were still there, it would be wriggling around, wouldn't it? It would be able to speak. Why would it lie still, continuing on in becoming? If there is demerit, it experiences suffering. When the khandhas are about to cease, it begins. Bhava, bhave sambhavanti – it is restless because it has no place to cling to, no place to go. Once it sees, in a flash it's gone, like taking a photograph. How could it remain? It's not that performing bang-sukun sends that person to heaven or nibbāna. It is performed for the living to see. The teachers want us to see, to contemplate: after all that clinging to this and that, what did we gain? What is there?

title: Ajahn Funn: See? Is our mind at ease? Sitting is comfortable. Kāya-lahutā (lightness of body), citta-lahutā (lightness of mind) — light in body and self,…
content:
Kāya-Lahutā, Citta-Lahutā: Lightness of Body and Mind – Diseases Dissolve

See? Is our mind at ease? Sitting is comfortable. Kāya-lahutā (lightness of body), citta-lahutā (lightness of mind) — light in body and self, light in form and physique. Diseases and dangers disappear, calamities and misfortunes disappear, defilements and nuisances disappear, evil and depravity disappear, misery and poverty disappear. Because our mind is not poor. Our mind has happiness, our mind has ease — not because of material goods or money, not because of a comfortable house or work. Our mind is at ease.

When our mind is at ease, then work is easy, study is easy, earning a living is easy, family is easy. When it is like that, people in the marketplace are at ease, the country is at ease. When we pass away (extinguish the aggregates), we dwell in ease. We do not need to think about past or future. The past is gone — good or bad has already happened. The future has not yet arrived. When the present is at ease, the future is at ease because this person is one who makes merit. This is called merit. Know and understand this. Virtue and goodness — know it.

title: Ajahn Funn: We must have virtue (sīla) and Dhamma. Sīla is the normalcy of body and mind. Sīla is a cool, calm, happy, at-ease heart…
content:
Sīla-Dhamma: Normalcy of Body and Mind – Calm, Happy, at Ease

We must have virtue (sīla) and Dhamma. Sīla is the normalcy of body and mind. Sīla is a cool, calm, happy, at-ease heart – that is called sīla-dhamma. If the mind is chaotic and distressed, it is not sīla nor Dhamma. A troubled, difficult mind is not sīla nor Dhamma. A body that is orderly and a mind that is orderly – that is sīla. Normal body, normal mind – that is the matter of sīla. Having happiness and comfort – that is the matter of sīla. An uncalm, chaotic, distressed mind is not sīla.

Therefore, the Buddha suppressed distress. That's how it is. Where does he suppress it? With what? Distress is burning with the fire of lust (rāga), burning with the fire of greed (lobha), burning with the fire of hatred (dosa). Ādittaṁ jātiyā jarā maraṇena – burning with birth, burning with aging, burning with sickness, burning with death. Now, if we suppress all suffering, then lust, greed, and hatred also cease. Our mind does not arise, does not age, and so it is not distressed. Whatever does not arise does not burn. When there is no more birth, there is no aging – so it does not burn.

title: Ajahn Funn: Then when you pass away, you are born in a bad place. Whatever a person is going to be, you must know now,…
content:
Good Destiny and Bad Destiny Are in the Mind – Not in the Sky

Then when you pass away, you are born in a bad place. Whatever a person is going to be, you must know now, see now, right now. No need to deceive anyone. Let each of us look. You may criticise: "I can't do that" or "I am not like this." But look and see clearly, truly, directly. What we call "kamma is one's own" – "my destiny is bad" – where is this bad destiny? Is it in the sky or the atmosphere? A good destiny – look: a good destiny is when we sit, our mind is peaceful and good, happy and comfortable in heart. That is a good destiny. Whatever you do, it's good. That is a good destiny. What is a bad destiny? The mind is disturbed, distressed – that is a bad destiny. When the mind is not good, whatever you do is not good, whatever you seek is not good, whatever you take is not good.

So when it's like this, quickly recollect Buddho and cut it off. That is why they say "cut off kamma, cut off enmity, wash away defilements and kamma" – not cut with a knife or sword, but use our mindfulness, Buddho, to cut it. "Wash away defilements and kamma" – how can it be washed? Not with water or soap. Rely on sitting in meditation; when our mind becomes peaceful, if our mind is not kamma, where could kamma come from? That's the explanation.

title: Ajahn Funn: As it is said: bhavābhave sambhavanti. Bhava means "becoming" or "realm of existence"; sambhavanti means "they arise in connection with" — with mental…
content:
Bhava and Jāti: Whatever Object We Take, We Will Be Reborn as That Thing

As it is said: bhavābhave sambhavanti. Bhava means "becoming" or "realm of existence"; sambhavanti means "they arise in connection with" — with mental objects, with perceptions. The matter is like this: dhammārammaṇa, kāmārammaṇa, rūpārammaṇa, saddārammaṇa, gandhārammaṇa, rasārammaṇa, phoṭṭhabbārammaṇa. Whatever object we take, we will be reborn as that thing.

Once born, it then ages. If we know and do not grasp anything at all, we are not born. When we are not born, what is there to age? When we do not age, what is there to get sick? When we do not get sick, what is there to die? When we do not die, what is there to suffer? See? It all ends — the end of realms of existence, the end of births. That is how it is.

But we do not end realms and births. We keep creating them endlessly. Now, from here on, each person focus and see. We do not want suffering, so we stop birth. Let the mind be still and neutral. Do not send it out. Now, contemplate so that you know. We say we do not want to create evil, kamma, danger, enmity, or unwholesomeness. Look: who is the kamma? Who is the danger? Who is the enmity? Kamma does not come from far away. Kāya-kamma, vacī-kamma, mano-kamma — they arise from the body, from speech, from the mind. Now, our bodily kamma is no more; our verbal kamma is no more; only mental kamma remains. Mental kamma is the act of attention, of thinking. Whatever we are recollecting, whatever feeling is in ourselves — we know that directly.

title: Ajahn Funn: Sitting listening to the Dhamma, gaining merit and wholesome qualities, we come to examine our own merit. Sit comfortably. Place your right leg…
content:
Establishing the Mind in Meditation: Buddho, Buddho

Sitting listening to the Dhamma, gaining merit and wholesome qualities, we come to examine our own merit. Sit comfortably. Place your right leg over your left, your right hand over your left. Keep your body straight. Assume a dignified, radiant posture with a bright smile. When your body is at ease, recollect your refuge: the virtues of the Buddha, the Dhamma, and the Noble Saṅgha as your shelter, your recollection, your object of respect, veneration, and homage. Recollect that the Buddha is in your heart, the Dhamma is in your heart, the Noble Saṅgha is in your heart. Be firmly confident in that, then recite the meditation word: “Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho; Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho; Buddho, Dhammo, Saṅgho” three times, then merge it into just “Buddho, Buddho.”

Close your eyes. Shut your mouth. Recollect inwardly: “Buddho” – knowing. Recollect inside your mind. Don’t move your tongue. Recollect in your mind: “Buddho” – where is that knowing? Then establish mindfulness right there. Let your eyes gaze there, your ears listen there. Why do you look? Why do you listen? We want to hear, we want to know, we want to see what merit and wholesomeness are like, what evil is like, what happiness is like, what suffering is like. This is what we want to know.

title: Ajahn Funn: Now, the duty of meditation (bhāvanā-vatta): The Buddha taught us to meditate, to go to the temple and listen to Dhamma. Meditation means…
content:
The Duty of Meditation: Selecting the Mind – From Animal Mind to Buddha Mind

Now, the duty of meditation (bhāvanā-vatta): The Buddha taught us to meditate, to go to the temple and listen to Dhamma. Meditation means contemplating and selecting within ourselves, selecting our own minds. The human mind has many states. Therefore, the Buddha taught us to select:

Manussatiracchāno – human body but animal mind. What is an animal mind? Lazy, sluggish; after eating it sleeps, does nothing, doesn’t know how to make merit or give gifts, doesn’t know how to pay respect, bow, or venerate anything.

Manussapeto – human body but peta (hungry ghost) mind. It has only anger and aversion in the mind – that’s a peta, isn’t it?

Manussanirayo – human body but hell-being mind. What is that like? A mind that is distressed, burning with suffering and torment – hell is like that.

Therefore, the Buddha taught us to meditate, to select and examine our own mind. When we see these things, we stop them, abandon them, let them go. Then we become manussamanusso – human body and human mind.

Manussadevo – human body but celestial being (deva) mind. What is that like? A mind that bows, pays respect, makes merit, gives gifts. A clear, bright mind that meditates, venerates, makes merit, gives gifts.

title: Ajahn Funn: When we do this kind of merit, it is called paṭipatti pūjā – the most excellent and supreme offering. Giving charity, making merit…
content:
Paṭipatti Pūjā: Sitting in Meditation Is the Supreme Offering

When we do this kind of merit, it is called paṭipatti pūjā – the most excellent and supreme offering. Giving charity, making merit – anything else compares to sitting in meditation like this? Now, look closely. Don't you know what is good? Don't you know when your own mind is not good? You want to be good, but your mind is not good – what will make you good? Therefore, as for merit and demerit, whatever they are, the mind comes first. The Pāli says: Manopubbaṅgamā dhammā, manoseṭṭhā manomayā – "Mind precedes all mental states; mind is their chief; they are mind-made." Merit and demerit – whatever they are – mind comes first. It is accomplished with the heart. Don't keep criticising that this is not good, that is not good. Look and purify it now. From now on, when our mind is good, we receive the results of goodness in the present and in the future. When our mind is not good, we receive the results in the present and in the future.

title: Ajahn Funn: When one sees like this, one abandons sakkāya-diṭṭhi. One no longer holds it to be a self. Then there is no greed, anger,…
content:
One Who Sees Dhamma Sees the Buddha and Enters Parinibbāna

When one sees like this, one abandons sakkāya-diṭṭhi. One no longer holds it to be a self. Then there is no greed, anger, or delusion. Our mind is calm, cool, light, and at ease. I have explained this for you to hear. Once you understand, from now on contemplate these things. Ah, we hold that we are a person. Where is it? The explanation has shown that it is like this. It points out and reveals within this very body of ours.

One who will attain happiness and progress, entering the Path and Fruition, contemplates right here. If we contemplate and see this Dhamma, see the saṅkhāra of our own body, that person sees Dhamma. One who sees Dhamma will see the Buddha's teaching, will see the Buddha, will know the Buddha. When one sees the Buddha, that person will enter parinibbāna, extinguishing suffering in saṃsāra, no longer wandering and revolving through birth and death. That is how it is. Therefore, let us all know, see, and enter.

title: Ajahn Funn: When our mind is established within, that is samādhi. Nowadays, people are looking for something permanent to rely on. But all things are…
content:
The End Is Beautiful: Paññā – Thorough Knowledge of Saṅkhāra

When our mind is established within, that is samādhi. Nowadays, people are looking for something permanent to rely on. But all things are impermanent. Therefore, the Buddha laid down the teaching: they are impermanent. Whatever is impermanent is dukkha. Whatever is dukkha is not self. Know this. We try to make it permanent. If our mind were permanent, not changing or moving about, it could be relied upon. But it cannot be relied upon; our mind goes here, we tell it to stay there, it goes there, we tell it to stay here — it wobbles, sways, and shifts.

This is why the Buddha laid down the practice of samādhi. Pariyosānakalyāṇaṃ — the end is beautiful, that is one who has paññā. Paññā is not just words. It is having thorough knowledge of conditioned phenomena (saṅkhāra), knowing the bodily saṅkhāra and mental saṅkhāra. Saṅkhāra — the concocting, the fabricating — whether it concocts as wholesome (kusala) or unwholesome (akusala). This is knowing saṅkhāra thoroughly, knowing our own consciousness. That is called paññā.
